---
title: "Why Beyond the Mean?"
subtitle: "Introduction to Quantile Regression"
author: "Tatjana Kecojević"
date: "24 June 2026"

format:
  revealjs:
    theme: serif
    slide-number: true
    logo: UoM_logos/UoM_logo_TAB_col_white_background.png
    title-slide: false
editor: visual
---

# Learning Objectives

By the end of this session you will be able to:

- Explain what OLS estimates
- Describe situations where mean effects may be insufficient
- Interpret quantiles and conditional quantiles
- Explain the motivation for quantile regression

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Do We Use Regression?

Researchers often ask:

- Does education increase earnings?
- How does socioeconomic status affect health?
- Do neighbourhood characteristics influence housing prices?
- Does parental background affect educational attainment?

Regression provides a framework for answering these questions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What Does Regression Allow Us to Do?

Regression models help researchers to:

- Describe relationships between variables
- Estimate the magnitude of effects
- Test theoretical expectations
- Predict outcomes

Regression is one of the most widely used tools in social science research.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Regression in Social Science Research

Regression models are among the most widely used statistical tools in the social sciences.

Researchers routinely use regression analysis to:

- Investigate relationships between variables
- Test theoretical expectations
- Estimate the effects of social, economic and demographic factors
- Predict outcomes for different groups and individuals

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

The most common approach for analysing relationships between variables is:

### Ordinary Least Squares Regression

OLS provides a useful summary of the relationship between predictors and outcomes by focusing on the **conditional mean** of the outcome variable.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A Simple Regression Model

Suppose we model wages as a function of years of education:

$$wage_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 education_i + \varepsilon_i$$

The coefficient $\beta_1$ represents the expected change in wages associated with an additional year of education.

### What exactly does OLS estimate?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What Does OLS Actually Estimate?

OLS is often described as estimating the relationship between X and Y.

More precisely, OLS estimates:

$$E(Y|X)$$

the **conditional mean** of the outcome variable given a set of predictors.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Is the Mean Useful?

The conditional mean provides a convenient summary of the relationship between predictors and outcomes.

For many research questions, the average effect is exactly what we want to know.

OLS is therefore an extremely valuable and widely used method.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## The Conditional Mean

For example:

- \(Y\) = hourly wage
- \(X\) = years of education

OLS estimates:

$$E(Wage|Education)$$

the average wage associated with different levels of education.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## But Is the Mean Always Enough?

OLS summarises the relationship between variables using a single average effect.

However, many social science questions involve heterogeneity. For example:

- Does education benefit low earners and high earners equally?
- Do policies affect everyone in the same way?
- Are some groups affected more than others?

To answer these questions, we may need to look beyond the mean.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Same Mean, Different Distributions

![](figures/same_mean_different_distributions.png)

Two groups can have approximately the same mean wage but very different distributions.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What Do We Learn?

Both groups have very similar average wages.

However:

- Group A is concentrated around the mean.
- Group B contains two distinct clusters of workers.

The mean captures only one aspect of a distribution.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Does This Matter?

Focusing only on averages can hide:

- Inequality
- Dispersion
- Distributional differences
- Heterogeneous effects

The mean is only one feature of a distribution.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## An Illustrative Example

Suppose policymakers are considering two policies designed to increase wages.

Both policies raise average wages by approximately:

### £2 per hour

Would we conclude that they have the same effect?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Identical Mean Effects, Different Distributional Effects

![](figures/policy_comparison.png)

Both policies increase average wages by approximately £2 per hour.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Interpreting the Policy Example

Although average wages increase by the same amount:

- Policy A benefits workers relatively evenly.
- Policy B concentrates gains among higher-paid workers.

The distributional consequences differ substantially.

### Who benefits matters.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Looking Beyond Averages

To understand these differences we need tools that describe:

- Lower outcomes
- Middle outcomes
- Upper outcomes

separately.

This motivates the use of quantiles.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## What Is a Quantile?

A quantile divides a distribution into ordered parts.

Common examples include:

- 25th percentile
- 50th percentile (median)
- 75th percentile

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Quantiles Divide a Distribution

![](figures/quantiles_divide_distribution.png)

The dashed lines indicate the 25th, 50th and 75th percentiles.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Quantiles?

Quantiles allow us to examine:

- Lower parts of the distribution
- The centre of the distribution
- Upper parts of the distribution

separately.

They provide a richer description than the mean alone.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Conditional Quantiles

OLS estimates:

$$E(Y|X)$$

Quantile regression estimates:

$$Q_{\tau}(Y|X)$$

where $(\tau)$ identifies a particular quantile.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Why Quantile Regression?

Quantile regression allows us to ask:

- Does education matter more for low earners?
- Does education matter more for high earners?
- Who benefits the most?
- Who benefits the least?

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Key Takeaways

- OLS estimates conditional means.
- Mean effects may conceal important heterogeneity.
- Similar means can correspond to very different distributions.
- Quantiles describe different parts of a distribution.
- Quantile regression allows us to move beyond the mean.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Next Section

### Quantiles and Conditional Quantiles

From averages to distributions.
